perturbation.original_text: The contractual partners undertake to treat confidentially any information they obtain, to use it only for the intended purpose of the particular assignment or project and not to make it di rectly or indirectly accessible to third parties. Furthermore, they undertake to place a duty of confidentiality on their employees and any sub -contractors that correspond to the one contained in this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: The contractual partners undertake to treat confidentially any information they obtain, to use it only for the intended purpose of the particular assignment or project and not to make it di rectly or indirectly accessible to third parties.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the sentence 'Furthermore, they undertake to place a duty of confidentiality on their employees and any sub -contractors that correspond to the one contained in this Agreement,' the contract fails to ensure that downstream parties are also bound by confidentiality. This creates a potential legal risk because Swiss law requires reasonable measures to protect trade secrets, including ensuring employees and subcontractors are also bound by confidentiality agreements.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Swiss Federal Act on Unfair Competition, Art. 6
perturbation.law_explanation: Article 6 of the Swiss Federal Act on Unfair Competition (UWG) prohibits the disclosure of trade secrets by employees or subcontractors. By omitting the requirement for the contractual partners to impose a duty of confidentiality on their employees and subcontractors, the agreement fails to comply with the necessary measures to safeguard trade secrets as outlined in Art. 6 UWG, thus potentially violating Swiss Law.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Fedlex DE: Vielen Dank, dass Sie sie die Website des Bundesrechts aufgerufen haben; sie ist nur mit einem Javascript-fähigen Browser verfügbar. Um diese Website optimal nutzen zu können, empfehlen wir Ihnen, einen aktuellen Browser zu verwenden. FR: Merci d'avoir rejoint le site du droit fédéral; celui-ci n'est disponible qu'avec un navigateur supportant javascript. Afin de profiter d'une expérience maximale sur ce site, nous vous conseillons d'utiliser un navigateur récent. IT: Grazie per aver scelto il sito web del diritto federale; questo è disponibile soltanto con un browser che supporta JavaScript. Per navigare in modo ottimale in questo sito, vi consigliamo di utilizzare un browser più recente. RM: Grazia fitg che Vus visitais la pagina d’internet dal dretg federal; ella è disponibla mo cun in navigatur che sustegna javascript. Per pudair far diever da questa pagina d’internet en moda optimala As recumandain nus d’utilisar in navigatur actual. EN: Thank you for visiting the federal law website; it is only fully accessible if you are using a JavaScript-capable browser. In order to be able to use all the features of this site, we recommend that you use an up-to-date browser. perturbation.justification: The original text ensures confidentiality extends to employees and subcontractors, while the changed text removes this requirement. The Perturbation Explanation highlights the legal risk in Swiss law regarding trade secret protection if employees and subcontractors aren't bound by confidentiality agreements. The Law Explanation also states this omission fails to comply with measures to safeguard trade secrets, potentially violating Swiss Law. Although the scraped snippets are not helpful, the combined explanations strongly suggest a failure to take necessary measures, creating a potential legal risk under Swiss law. Therefore, a contradiction exists.

perturbation.original_text: Upon completion of an assignment or project, the contractual partners undertake to destroy immediately any information or objects given to them that they or any sub -contractors may have, unless such information and property are required for any outstanding or future follow -up activities under the mutual business partnership. The destruction of i nformation in electronic form also applies to e- mails, data storage media such as USB sticks, local hard disks, servers and back -up copies.
perturbation.changed_text: Upon completion of an assignment or project, the contractual partners undertake to destroy any information or objects given to them that they or any sub -contractors may have, unless such information and property are required for any outstanding or future follow -up activities under the mutual business partnership.
perturbation.explanation: By removing 'immediately' and the part of the text regarding destruction of information in electronic form, the contract weakens the data protection obligations. This could create legal risk under Swiss data protection laws, which mandate that personal data must be destroyed when it is no longer required.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Swiss Federal Act on Data Protection, Art. 5
perturbation.law_explanation: Article 5 of the Swiss Federal Act on Data Protection (FADP) requires that personal data be destroyed as soon as it is no longer necessary for the purpose for which it was collected. By omitting the word 'immediately' and specifics around electronic destruction, the revised text creates ambiguity and weakens the obligation to ensure timely and complete destruction of data, thus conflicting with the data protection principles enshrined in Art. 5 FADP.

perturbation.justification: The original contract language contains stronger data protection obligations by including the word 'immediately' for data destruction and specifying electronic forms of information. The changed text weakens these obligations by omitting 'immediately' and the clause specifically addressing electronic data. This omission creates a legal risk because, according to the Law Explanation, Article 5 of the Swiss Federal Act on Data Protection (FADP) requires personal data to be destroyed as soon as it is no longer necessary. By removing 'immediately' and the specific mention of electronic destruction, the revised text introduces ambiguity and makes it less clear when and how data must be destroyed, thus potentially conflicting with data protection principles.

perturbation.original_text: This agreement shall take effect as soon as it has been signed by both parties and shall be valid for the duration of the collaboration or until this agreement is replaced by another agreement. The obligation to confidentiality shall apply for a period of two years beyond the end of the collaboration.
perturbation.changed_text: This agreement shall take effect as soon as it has been signed by both parties and shall be valid for the duration of the collaboration or until this agreement is replaced by another agreement.
perturbation.explanation: By removing 'The obligation to confidentiality shall apply for a period of two years beyond the end of the collaboration', the contract eliminates the post-collaboration confidentiality obligation. Swiss law requires reasonable protection of trade secrets, even after the formal business relationship ends. Omitting this clause opens up legal risks if confidential information is disclosed after the collaboration concludes.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Swiss Federal Act on Unfair Competition, Art. 6
perturbation.law_explanation: Article 6 of the Swiss Federal Act on Unfair Competition (UWG) protects trade secrets. Omitting the two-year post-collaboration confidentiality clause weakens the protection of confidential information after the end of the collaboration. This conflicts with the principles of Art. 6 UWG, which requires safeguarding trade secrets and preventing their unauthorized disclosure or exploitation even after a business relationship has ended.

perturbation.justification: The original text included a confidentiality obligation for two years post-collaboration, which was completely removed in the changed text. The Law Explanation states that this omission conflicts with the principles of Art. 6 UWG, which requires safeguarding trade secrets and preventing their unauthorized disclosure or exploitation even after a business relationship has ended. While the scraped snippets do not directly support or contradict this, the Perturbation Explanation highlights that Swiss law requires reasonable protection of trade secrets, even after the formal business relationship ends. Since the original text contains an obligation that was removed, and the explanation says the removal undermines existing law, the modified text creates a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Changes or additions to this Agreement (including the waiver of this provision) must be made in writing.
perturbation.changed_text: Changes or additions to this Agreement must be made in writing.
perturbation.explanation: By removing '(including the waiver of this provision)', the contract text makes it unclear if the written form requirement can itself be waived only in writing. This is problematic under Swiss law which generally requires waivers to be explicit and unambiguous, particularly for essential contractual elements.
perturbation.law_explanation: Article 16 of the Swiss Code of Obligations (CO) requires explicit declarations for waivers of rights. By removing the clarification regarding the waiver of the written form requirement, the amended text introduces ambiguity. A Swiss court might find that a verbal agreement could waive the written form requirement, thereby undermining the original intent and increasing the risk of disputes over contract modifications. By not stating 'including the waiver of this provision' can only be done in writing, you could argue the initial agreement can be altered verbally, which can contradict Swiss Law.

perturbation.justification: The removal of '(including the waiver of this provision)' from the original text creates a legal contradiction under Swiss law. The original text explicitly stated that any changes to the agreement, including the waiver of the written form requirement, must be in writing. By omitting this clarification, the changed text introduces ambiguity, potentially allowing for verbal waivers of the written form requirement. This contradicts the principle that waivers, particularly for essential contractual elements, should be explicit and unambiguous. Article 16 of the Swiss Code of Obligations (CO) requires explicit declarations for waivers of rights. The absence of the phrase could lead a court to interpret that the written form requirement itself can be waived verbally. This creates a loophole, undermining the original intent of ensuring all modifications, including waivers, are documented in writing. There are no scraped law snippets to further support this.
